body_txt: Whether it be a simple game or life-or-death stakes, some people not only don't mind losing, but handle said loss with grace and dignity. This speaks well of their character, especially if they were initially presented as antagonistic. It could be because they are putting things into perspective, particularly sportsmanlike, or are just naturally mature and accept loss as a part of life. A common application is to a villain who has been bested by the heroes willingly laying down their sword. Perhaps the villain is sufficiently well-intentioned or rational to quit fighting , or a Noble Demon who respects the heroes' strength. It might indicate a genuine camaraderie between them and their heroic nemesis. It might even cause them to join the hero's side. Similarly, The Rival and similar antagonistic character archetypes might start as sore losers or winners when they clash with the hero early on, but Character Development can spur them into accepting the heroes' prowess. Another common situation for graceful loss is in matters of the heart . A Romantic Runner-Up who bows out when they realize their Love Interest doesn't prefer them is usually characterized as kind and understanding. Since they've shown themselves to be a nice person, just not right for the hero, they are ripe for a Last-Minute Hookup or Pair the Spares situation at the end of the story. Related to A Taste of Defeat , Face Death with Dignity , Sportsmanship Aesop , and Humble Pie . Contrast Sore Loser , Competition Freak , Unsportsmanlike Gloating , I Surrender, Suckers! (its absolute opposite), and Rage Quit . Compare and contrast with Defiant to the End , where the loser retains their dignity but refuses to admit defeat. Compare Graceful Winner , when the victor is gracious to the loser, regardless of their behavior. May overlap with Actually Pretty Impressive , if the loser admires the victor’s success.
